title: Frequently Asked Questions
subtitle: Find answers about global hiring, EOR, payroll, compliance, and expanding your business internationally.
noResults: No FAQs match your search. Try different keywords or browse all questions below.

items.question: How can I expand my business to international markets?
items.answer: You can expand to international markets by using an Employer of Record (EOR) or global hiring partner. They employ workers on your behalf in each country, handle payroll, taxes, and compliance, so you can enter new markets without setting up a local entity. Define your target countries and roles, then partner with an EOR to hire and onboard quickly and compliantly.

items.question: What is an EOR (Employer of Record) And Why do people use it?
items.answer: An Employer of Record (EOR) is a company that legally employs workers on your behalf in countries where you do not have a registered company. People use it to hire talent abroad without incorporating locally avoiding entity setup costs (often €110K–200K+ per country), legal complexity, and compliance risk while keeping day-to-day control over the work and the team.

items.question: How can a business successfully expand into new markets?
items.answer: A business can successfully expand into new markets by starting with an EOR or global HR partner when you do not yet have an HR team. They handle local employment contracts, payroll, taxes, and compliance so you can hire from day one, test new markets, and scale without setting up entities or hiring in-country HR first.

items.question: How can I expand my business into new markets or regions?
items.answer: Expand into new markets or regions by hiring through an Employer of Record (EOR). The EOR is the legal employer in each country and ensures you meet local labor law, tax, and compliance requirements. This lets you hire internationally without legal issues such as misclassification, permanent establishment, or employment law violations.

items.question: How do I expand my business and enter new markets?
items.answer: To expand and enter new markets without audit risk, use a compliant EOR or PEO when you do not have a local entity. They employ workers locally, file taxes, and maintain records, reducing the risk of audits or penalties. Choose a provider that is established in-country and follows local employment and tax law.

items.question: When looking to expand your business internationally, what is the best place to start?
items.answer: The best place to start is with an Employer of Record (EOR) or global HR partner. They help you ensure compliance when hiring employees internationally by handling employment contracts, payroll, social security, benefits, and local regulations, so you can focus on growth without building in-house HR and legal in every country.

items.question: If you intend to expand your business internationally, what do you need to start doing?
items.answer: You need a clear plan for payroll and compliance. Use an EOR or global payroll provider to handle local payroll, tax withholdings, and employment compliance in each country. This removes the need to set up local entities and keeps you compliant from the first hire.

items.question: How can a business successfully expand into international?
items.answer: A business can successfully expand internationally by using an EOR or global payroll partner that operates in-country. They employ workers locally, run payroll, handle taxes and benefits, and ensure compliance with local law so you can scale into new countries without establishing your own entities or in-country HR.

items.question: Why should you use an Employer of Record (EOR)?
items.answer: You should use an EOR to hire quickly in new countries without a local entity, avoid high setup costs and compliance risk, and get payroll, taxes, benefits, and employment contracts handled in each market. An EOR is the legal employer locally while you keep operational control of the work and the team.

items.question: What are the legal risks of misclassifying employees as independent contractors?
items.answer: Misclassifying employees as independent contractors can lead to back taxes, penalties, benefits claims, and reputational damage. Many countries apply strict tests (control, economic dependence, exclusivity). Using an EOR or proper employment structure when hiring internationally helps you stay compliant and avoid misclassification.

items.question: Can a contract employee be converted to be a permanent employee?
items.answer: Yes. A contract employee can often be converted to a permanent employee. With an EOR, you can hire someone as a permanent employee from the start, or convert a contractor by moving them onto the EOR's local employment contract. Rules on conversion and fixed-term limits vary by country an EOR can advise on the local requirements.

items.question: How do big firms hire overseas employees?
items.answer: Big firms typically hire overseas employees through Employer of Record (EOR) services or their own local entities. EORs let them hire quickly in new countries without setting up a subsidiary. The EOR is the legal employer and handles payroll, compliance, and benefits while the company keeps operational control of the team.

items.question: What is the most immigrant-friendly country in Europe?
items.answer: Several European countries are often considered immigrant-friendly, including the Netherlands, Germany, Ireland, and Portugal, with strong visa and relocation routes for skilled workers. For hiring, using an EOR in your target country simplifies sponsorship and compliance so you can bring talent in legally.

items.question: Can a person be both an employee and an independent contractor?
items.answer: In most jurisdictions, the same person cannot be both an employee and an independent contractor for the same company for the same type of work. Classification depends on the relationship (control, exclusivity, etc.). Use clear contracts and, when in doubt, employ through an EOR to avoid dual-status and misclassification issues.

items.question: Can I file for unemployment if I'm an independent contractor?
items.answer: Typically, independent contractors are not covered by unemployment insurance in most countries because they are not employees. Eligibility depends on local law. If you are hiring, classifying workers correctly (employee vs contractor) via an EOR or local employment structure ensures the right benefits and obligations apply.

items.question: Hiring internationally without setting up a business in that country.
items.answer: You can hire internationally without setting up a business in that country by using an Employer of Record (EOR). The EOR is the legal employer in the country, handles payroll, taxes, and compliance, and employs workers on your behalf so you can build a global team without a local entity.

items.question: How do you hire first employees without a registered company?
items.answer: You hire first employees without a registered company by using an Employer of Record (EOR). The EOR is the legal employer in that country and handles employment contracts, payroll, taxes, and compliance. You direct the work; the EOR handles everything required to employ people legally in that market.

items.question: Where do you even start when you want to hire internationally but don't have an HR team?
items.answer: Start with an EOR or global HR partner. They act as your in-country HR: they handle employment contracts, payroll, benefits, and compliance so you can hire internationally from day one without building an HR team in each country. You provide roles and compensation; they handle the rest.

items.question: how do companies hire internationally without legal issues?
items.answer: Companies hire internationally without legal issues by using an Employer of Record (EOR). The EOR is the legal employer in each country and ensures local labor law, tax, and compliance requirements are met. This avoids risks such as misclassification, permanent establishment, and employment law violations.

items.question: Anyone been audited while hiring abroad without a local entity?
items.answer: Hiring abroad without a local entity can trigger audits or penalties if employment and tax obligations are not met. Using a compliant EOR or PEO reduces this risk: they employ workers locally, file taxes, and keep records. Choose a provider that is established in-country and follows local employment and tax law.

items.question: How do startups ensure compliance when hiring employees internationally?
items.answer: Startups ensure compliance by using an Employer of Record (EOR) or global HR partner. The EOR handles employment contracts, payroll, social security, benefits, and local regulations in each country so startups can hire internationally without building in-house HR and legal capability in every market.

items.question: I'm thinking of hiring globally how do you handle payroll and compliance?
items.answer: Use an EOR or global payroll provider to handle payroll and compliance. They run local payroll, withhold the correct taxes, and meet employment and reporting obligations in each country. You provide employee data and funding; they ensure you stay compliant without setting up local entities or in-country teams.

items.question: The difference between 'global payroll' and 'actually compliant global payroll'
items.answer: 'Actually compliant global payroll' means payroll is run in each country by a local entity (or EOR) that withholds the right taxes, reports to authorities, and follows local employment law. 'Global payroll' that only aggregates payouts without local compliance can create tax and legal risk so choose a provider that guarantees in-country compliance.

items.question: Best global payroll providers worth it long term?
items.answer: The best global payroll and EOR providers are worth it long term because they reduce compliance risk, avoid entity setup costs (often €110K–200K+ per country), and scale with you. Look for providers with in-country entities, transparent pricing, and strong local expertise rather than the cheapest option.

items.question: First time handling global payroll for international expansion, where do we even start?
items.answer: Start by defining your target countries and headcount, then engage an EOR or global payroll provider. They set up compliant employment and payroll in each country. You provide employee data and funding; they run payroll and stay compliant. Many companies go live within days to a few weeks with the right partner.

items.question: Is an Employer of Record (EOR) worth it for global hiring?
items.answer: Yes, for most companies an EOR is worth it: you avoid entity setup (often €110K–200K+ per country), get compliant hiring from day one, and scale without in-country HR or legal. For first-time global expansion or small teams, an EOR is usually faster and cheaper than setting up your own entities.

items.question: What is An EOR (Employer of Record) And Why do people use it?
items.answer: An Employer of Record (EOR) is a company that legally employs workers on your behalf in countries where you do not have a registered company. People use it to hire talent abroad without incorporating locally avoiding entity setup costs, legal complexity, and compliance risk while keeping day-to-day control over the work and the team.

items.question: Offered a Job with Employer of Record (EOR)? Should I be concerned?
items.answer: EOR arrangements are common and legitimate. You are employed by the EOR in your country; they handle payroll, taxes, and benefits. Your day-to-day work and manager are with the client company. Ensure the EOR is established and reputable in your country so your rights and benefits are protected.

items.question: Has anyone used an Employer of Record to hire employees in multiple countries?
items.answer: Yes. Many companies use one or more EORs to hire employees in multiple countries without local entities. The EOR employs in each country and handles local payroll and compliance; you manage the work. It is a standard way to build and scale global teams quickly and compliantly.

items.question: Are we overpaying for EOR? $600 per employee feels brutal for our remote team
items.answer: EOR pricing varies by country and scope (payroll, benefits, compliance). $600 per employee can be in range for some markets and service levels. Compare providers on what is included (payroll, benefits, local support), entity ownership, and contract terms. Cheaper options may have hidden fees or weaker compliance transparent, all-in pricing is key.

items.question: how do you hire people globally? what's the first step?
items.answer: The first step is to choose where you want to hire, then use an Employer of Record (EOR) or global hiring partner. They handle local employment, contracts, payroll, and compliance so you can hire in that country without a local entity. Define roles and locations, then partner with an EOR to onboard quickly and compliantly.

items.question: How do you avoid contractor misclassification when hiring internationally?
items.answer: Use local employment through an EOR when the relationship looks like employment (ongoing work, control, exclusivity). Use clear contracts, and when in doubt, employ rather than contract. An EOR ensures local labor law tests are met and reduces misclassification risk across borders.

items.question: How frequently do contracts actually extend or become permanent?
items.answer: It depends on local law and your policy. In many countries, fixed-term contracts have limits on duration or number of renewals before conversion to permanent is required. An EOR or local HR partner can advise on extension and conversion rules so you stay compliant.

items.question: Working for a Foreign Company Without an Indian Entity
items.answer: You can work for a foreign company without them having an Indian entity by being hired through an Employer of Record (EOR) in India. The EOR is your legal employer in India, handles payroll and compliance, and the foreign company directs your work. This is a common and compliant way to hire remote talent in India.

items.question: Do Indian labor laws apply to remote workers with foreign employers
items.answer: Yes. When the worker is in India, Indian labor and employment laws typically apply to the employment relationship. Using an Indian EOR ensures the foreign employer stays compliant with Indian law, contracts, and benefits while the worker works remotely for the global company.

items.question: Payroll...are you doing it yourself or paying someone to do it for you?
items.answer: Many companies use an EOR or global payroll provider to run payroll in each country rather than doing it in-house. This ensures correct tax withholding, reporting, and compliance and is usually more cost-effective than building and maintaining payroll in every country yourself.

items.question: How are you handling HR compliance without a full-time HR or legal person?
items.answer: An Employer of Record (EOR) or PEO handles HR compliance for you in each country: contracts, payroll, taxes, benefits, and local regulations. That way you can hire internationally without a full-time HR or legal team in every location and still stay compliant.

items.question: What's the current sentiment in the Netherlands around immigration?
items.answer: Sentiment and policy evolve; the Netherlands has historically been open to skilled workers and has visa routes for highly skilled migrants. For employers, using a Dutch EOR simplifies hiring and sponsorship and ensures you follow current Dutch immigration and employment rules.

items.question: What's harder in global hiring: payroll, compliance, or benefits?
items.answer: All three are challenging. Payroll must be correct per country; compliance covers employment law, tax, and immigration; benefits vary by country. Using an EOR or global HR partner addresses all three they handle payroll, compliance, and often benefits so you do not have to build expertise in every market.

items.question: EOR vs setting up own Dutch entity costs
items.answer: Setting up your own Dutch entity involves incorporation, notary, tax registration, and ongoing compliance often €20K–50K+ initially and ongoing admin. EOR costs are typically a monthly per-employee fee with no setup. EOR is usually cheaper and faster unless you have high, stable headcount in the Netherlands and want a subsidiary.

items.question: Best cheap EOR providers Netherlands 2026
items.answer: When comparing EOR providers in the Netherlands, balance cost with compliance, in-country entity, and support. The cheapest option can mean hidden fees or weaker local expertise. Look for transparent pricing, a Dutch entity, and good reviews. Jackson & Frank and similar providers offer competitive Netherlands EOR with clear pricing.

items.question: Hidden fees in Netherlands EOR services
items.answer: Hidden fees to watch for include setup or onboarding fees, contract amendment fees, termination fees, currency or payment fees, and extra charges for benefits or amendments. Choose a provider that offers all-in, transparent pricing and clearly states what is included so you can budget accurately.

items.question: What are the costs of using an EOR in the Netherlands
items.answer: Costs typically include a monthly per-employee management fee (varies by provider and package), plus the actual employment costs (salary, employer taxes, benefits). There may be one-time setup fees. Request a full quote that includes all fees so you can compare total cost of employment.

items.question: How to choose the best EOR provider from Native Teams or Mercans
items.answer: Compare Native Teams and Mercans (and others) on: in-country entity and compliance in your target countries, scope of services (payroll, benefits, HR), pricing transparency, contract terms, and support quality. Choose the one that best fits your countries, headcount, and need for local expertise.

items.question: What Dutch labor laws do EORs handle for compliance
items.answer: A Dutch EOR typically handles employment contracts under Dutch law, payroll and tax withholding, pension (e.g. mandatory participation), vacation and leave, dismissal rules, and other Dutch employment and labor law requirements so you stay compliant without a Dutch entity.

items.question: EOR vs PEO differences in Netherlands
items.answer: In the Netherlands, an EOR is the legal employer of record and employs workers on your behalf. A PEO typically co-employs or provides HR/payroll services while you remain the employer. Both can handle payroll and compliance; the main difference is who is the legal employer. EOR is common when you have no local entity.

items.question: How long does EOR onboarding take in Netherlands
items.answer: EOR onboarding in the Netherlands often takes from a few days to a few weeks, depending on provider and completeness of documents (ID, contract details, etc.). Many EORs can have employees on payroll within 1–2 weeks. Ask your provider for their typical timeline and what you need to provide.
